title: How to Write a Solid Conclusion That Isn't Just Repeating Yourself
author: Tklesson Team
content:
'In conclusion...' 'To summarize...' 'As I have shown...' These are the phrases students reach for when they've run out of ideas. The conclusion is the last thing your teacher reads. It's your final chance to leave an impression. Don't waste it on repetition.

What a Conclusion Should Do

A good conclusion does three things:
1. Reminds the reader of your thesis (but in fresh words)
2. Synthesizes your main points (shows how they fit together, not just lists them)
3. Leaves the reader with something to think about (a question, a implication, a call to action)

It does not introduce new evidence. It does not apologize ('I'm not an expert, but...'). It does not just repeat the introduction.

The Synthesis, Not Summary, Rule

A summary says: 'I argued X, then Y, then Z.' A synthesis says: 'X, Y, and Z together show that...' The synthesis connects the dots. It answers the 'so what?' question.

Bad conclusion: 'This essay discussed the causes of World War I, including militarism, alliances, and nationalism.'
Good conclusion: 'Militarism, alliances, and nationalism did not cause World War I separately — they interacted. Each fed the others, creating a chain reaction that made war almost inevitable.'

The good conclusion shows how the points relate. That's synthesis.

The 'So What?' Test

After you write your conclusion, ask: 'So what? Why does this matter?' If your conclusion doesn't answer that question, keep writing.

A good conclusion leaves the reader thinking about the broader implications. How does your argument change the way we see the topic? What should the reader do with this information? Why should anyone care?

Strategies for a Strong Ending

The full circle method: Return to the anecdote or example from your introduction, but with new insight. 'The blinking cursor that terrified me at the start of this essay now looks like an invitation.'

The broader implications method: Zoom out. How does your argument apply to a larger context? 'This pattern of procrastination doesn't just affect test scores — it shapes careers, relationships, and self-worth.'

The call to action method: Tell the reader what to do next. 'The next time you face a blank page, don't wait for inspiration. Start writing garbage. The good stuff will follow.'

The question method: End with a question that lingers. 'If active recall is so effective, why do most students still re-read their notes?'

What to Avoid

• 'In conclusion' (it's lazy and everyone uses it)
• Restating your thesis word-for-word (use fresh language)
• Introducing a new argument (save it for another essay)
• Apologizing ('This essay only scratched the surface')
• Overused clichés ('The future is in our hands')

A Simple Conclusion Template

Use this formula to get started, then make it your own:

Sentence 1: Restate your thesis in fresh words.
Sentence 2-3: Synthesize your main points (show how they connect).
Sentence 4: State the broader implication or leave a final thought.

Example: 'Cramming doesn't work because it fights against how memory actually functions. Sleep, spaced repetition, and active recall aren't optional extras — they're essential parts of studying. The next time you're tempted to pull an all-nighter, remember: your brain needs rest to learn.'

The conclusion is your final word. Make it count. End strong, not just end.
